text:
Higher education research is still a relatively small and ill-defined research area. The borders are imprecise: they differentiate between those dedicated to the field on a professional basis and those who make research on higher education an exception in their overall academic or professional engagement. They are similarly imprecise as regards personnel: one is not always certain whether someone working in the higher education research area is a scientist or a consultant, a manager or an expert in policy preparation. In Europe, the national conditions under which higher education research is carried out still differ significantly from one another, though certain countries are solidly anchored in the field, while in other countries the area of higher education research is still marginal both in terms of themes and institutional locations. Normally, these differences are also reflected in the national focus of higher education research.

Although European research output is likely to be heavily influenced by a wide range of factors - not least of which is the more general use of the English language as the common language of world communication - there is clearly more multilateral communication between countries today than there was, for example, in the 1980s. Researchers indicate that a considerable part of their networks result from their own initiatives; in addition, the impact of Bologna-process-related research on the European dimension in higher education may contribute to the rise in European comparative research. As has been the case in a considerable part of the sector for many years, European support to research where priorities are 'targeted' or policy-oriented and appears to be a complex and multifaceted and hence a potential threat to researcher's freedom of scholarly judgement, but is also seen as a unique source of opportunity for engaging in serious high-level dialogue that contributes to high quality data. This work is accordingly an important factor in the evolution of higher education research itself, as well as the policy instruments that guide it.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
